questions.question: Explain with an example how you would use a Python dictionary to store and retrieve temperature and humidity data from multiple IoT sensors.
keyPoints: Define dictionary structure with sensor IDs as keys
Show nested dictionary for multiple readings per sensor
Demonstrate retrieval using sensor_id['temperature']
Explain O(1) lookup benefit over lists

questions.question: Describe the differences between lists, tuples, and sets in the context of IoT data storage. Give a specific IoT use case for each.
keyPoints: List: mutable, ordered - for accumulating sensor readings
Tuple: immutable, hashable - for fixed device configurations
Set: unique elements - for tracking unique device IDs
Compare memory usage and performance characteristics

questions.question: Write Python code to find the average of the last 10 temperature readings from a sensor data stream. Explain the data structure used.
keyPoints: Use collections.deque with maxlen=10
Demonstrate automatic buffer management
Show averaging calculation
Explain O(1) operations for stream processing

questions.question: How would you handle missing sensor data in Python? Explain with code example using appropriate data types.
keyPoints: Use None to represent missing values
Show conditional checking with 'is None'
Demonstrate default value fallback
Explain importance for IoT reliability

questions.question: Convert the following JSON-like sensor data to Python data structures and extract the average temperature: data = {'sensors': [{'id': 'S1', 'temp': 22}, {'id': 'S2', 'temp': 25}, {'id': 'S3', 'temp': 24}]}
keyPoints: Convert JSON to dictionary
Iterate through list of sensor dictionaries
Extract temp values
Calculate average
Handle edge cases

questions.question: Explain the concept of shallow copy vs deep copy in Python. Why is this important when processing nested IoT sensor data?
keyPoints: Define shallow copy: new container, same references
Define deep copy: completely independent copy
Show code example with nested sensor data
Explain how modifying copy affects original
Recommend deepcopy() for IoT data integrity

questions.question: Design a Python data structure to manage a smart building IoT system with 50 temperature sensors, 30 humidity sensors, and 20 light sensors. Each sensor reports readings every minute. The system needs to: (a) Store last 1 hour of historical data (b) Quickly look up any sensor's current reading (c) Track unique alerts generated (d) Calculate rolling averages efficiently. Explain your choice of data structures with time complexity analysis.
keyPoints: Use nested dictionaries: {sensor_id: deque(maxlen=60)} for historical data
Use separate dictionary for current readings: O(1) lookup
Use set for unique alerts
Use deque with maxlen for rolling averages
Analyze O(1), O(n) operations for each requirement
Consider memory efficiency

questions.question: Write a complete Python program for an IoT weather station that: (a) Simulates temperature readings from 3 sensors (b) Stores readings with timestamps (c) Converts Celsius to Fahrenheit (d) Detects and stores unique alert conditions (temperature > 35°C or < 5°C) (e) Provides function to get average temperature of all sensors. Use appropriate Python data structures and explain your design choices.
keyPoints: Use list of dictionaries for sensor readings
Use tuple for timestamped data (timestamp, value)
Use set for unique alerts
Implement functions with proper data flow
Handle edge cases and missing data
Show sample output

questions.question: Compare array, list, and deque data structures in Python for handling streaming IoT sensor data. Write code demonstrating each and analyze their performance for: (i) Appending new readings (ii) Removing oldest readings (iii) Accessing elements by index (iv) Finding maximum value. Which would you recommend for real-time IoT applications and why?
keyPoints: Compare append performance: list O(1), deque O(1), array O(1)
Compare pop(0): list O(n), deque O(1), array O(n)
Compare index access: all O(1)
Compare max(): list O(n), array O(n), use numpy for efficiency
Recommend deque for streaming with maxlen
Consider numpy arrays for numerical processing
